content.subtitle: Overview
items: Past simple and past continuous forms can describe unreal or hypothetical meaning in the present, future or in general statements.
Past perfect simple and past perfect continuous can describe unreal situations in the past that did not actually happen.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: With I/he/she/it after these unreal structures, both was and were may appear.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: In formal written English, were is often preferred, and it is the fixed choice in the phrase If I were you ...

content.subtitle: Use
items: For unlikely or imaginary present/future/general conditions: I wouldn't sign the contract unless I **was** absolutely sure of the terms.
For unreal past conditions and results: If I **had checked** the timetable, I would have arrived earlier.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To discuss unlikely present, future or general situations: Supposing you **were offered** a year abroad, would you accept?
To discuss unreal past situations: Imagine you **had taken** that role last year. How different would things be now?

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: A present tense after these words often suggests a more realistic possibility; a past form suggests lower probability.
examples: Supposing you **become** self-employed, how will your routine change? (more likely)
Supposing you **became** self-employed, how would your routine change? (less likely)

content.subtitle: Use
items: For unreal comparisons in the present/future/in general: He talks as if he **were** in charge of the whole project.
For unreal comparisons about the past: They reacted as though they **had never heard** the news before.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: After a present or present perfect main verb, a past form after as if/as though usually marks the comparison as unreal.
If the comparison is considered possible, a present or present perfect form can follow as if/as though.
After a past main verb, past forms after as if/as though are normal whether the comparison is real or unreal.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To make questions and requests sound softer and more polite: I was wondering if you could send me the updated file.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To say something should happen now or very soon: It's about time we **made** a final decision.

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: After it's time, both past simple and past continuous can be used with very similar meaning.
items: A full infinitive can follow it's time, but not the stronger forms it's high time / it's about time.

content.subtitle: Use
items: To express preference about another person's present/future/general action: I'd rather you **didn't mention** this yet.
To express preference about another person's past action: We'd sooner she **had told** us earlier.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Use a past form after would rather/sooner only when the subject changes; with the same subject, use an infinitive structure.
examples: ✓ I'd prefer **to stay** at home tonight.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Wishes about now, the future or in general: I wish I **had** more free time during the week.
Wishes about the past: If only we **had left** earlier, we wouldn't have missed the train.

content.subtitle: Other structures with wish / if only
items: Wish / If only + would → criticism or a desire for someone/something to change
Wish / If only + could → wish about ability or permission
Wish / If only + could + perfect infinitive → wish about past ability/permission
Wish + full infinitive → very formal expression of intention
Wish + noun → fixed expressions of goodwill

content.subtitle: Important Notes
items: Would is normally avoided when the subject of wish and would is the same person.
Use hope (not wish) for expected or likely future outcomes.
